coredata.dc:title: Efficient color-tunable room temperature phosphorescence through carbon dot confinement in urea crystals
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chao
Liang, Hao
Bai, Songchao
Zhu, Jinyang
Chen, Zhaojin
Yang, Gang
Zhu, Yongsheng
coredata.dc:description: Color-tunable phosphorescence has received extensive attention in recent years. Herein, a facile approach for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) in urea crystals (referred to as uCDs) is reported. Excitation dependence under ambient conditions and multicolor room temperature phosphorescence (RTP), changing from yellow–green (570 nm) to orange (610 nm) at excitation wavelengths of 365 and 460 nm, is observed in the synthesized uCDs. The multicolor emission can be attributed to the existence of multiple emitting centers in the uCDs and it is determined that hydrogen bonding plays a critical role in achieving RTP. High intensity phosphorescence was achieved by external heating of the prepared uCDs@150, which forms a tighter rigid urea matrix and results in an attained lifetime of 281 ms. Based on these unique color-tunable properties, the synthesized uCDs@150 have potential applications in anticounterfeiting and light-emitting diode technologies. This study not only proposes a strategy to prepare photo-stimulated multicolor RTP materials, but also reveals the potential of CDs in exploiting novel optical materials with unique properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Multi-color emission
Information security
Light-emitting diode

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of uCDs heat treated at 150 °C (uCDs@150) and the process of self-doping for color-tunable phosphorescence in uCDs@150.

Structural characterization of uCDs: (a) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and (b) HR TEM images; (c) size distribution; (d) XRD, (e) FT-IR, and (f) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra; high-resolution XPS fitting results for the (g) C 1s, (h) N 1s, and (i) O 1s spectra.

Investigation of uCDs color-tunable phosphorescence mechanism: (a) photographs of phosphorescence with different delay times at different excitation wavelengths; (b) phosphorescence excitation spectra obtained by monitoring different emission wavelengths; (c) absorption spectra; (d) time-resolved decay curves excited at 365 and 460 nm under ambient conditions, and (e) corresponding CIE coordinates. (f) Proposed uCDs excitation-dependent FL and RTP emission processes.

(a) Photographs of uCDs@150 powder irradiated with different wavelengths (365 and 460 nm) and at different delay times after irradiation was switched off. (b) Phosphorescence excitation spectra of uCDs@150 obtained by monitoring at different emission wavelengths. (c) Time-resolved decay curves excited at 365 and 460 nm under ambient conditions. (d) Phosphorescence intensity of uCDs and uCDs@150 at 365 nm and 460 nm excitation conditions.
Proposed mechanism for excitation-dependent phosphorescence of (a) uCDs and (b) uCDs@150.

(a) Images of “NYNU” patterns fabricated using uCDs@150 under on and off conditions at different excitation wavelengths (365 and 460 nm). (b) Photographs of afterglow emission from integrated LED devices after turned off.

head.ce:title: Efficient color-tunable room temperature phosphorescence through carbon dot confinement in urea crystals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-Carbon & Environmental Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Centre for International Cooperation on Designer Low-Carbon & Environmental Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Color-tunable phosphorescence has received extensive attention in recent years. Herein, a facile approach for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) in urea crystals (referred to as uCDs) is reported. Excitation dependence under ambient conditions and multicolor room temperature phosphorescence (RTP), changing from yellow–green (570 nm) to orange (610 nm) at excitation wavelengths of 365 and 460 nm, is observed in the synthesized uCDs. The multicolor emission can be attributed to the existence of multiple emitting centers in the uCDs and it is determined that hydrogen bonding plays a critical role in achieving RTP. High intensity phosphorescence was achieved by external heating of the prepared uCDs@150, which forms a tighter rigid urea matrix and results in an attained lifetime of 281 ms. Based on these unique color-tunable properties, the synthesized uCDs@150 have potential applications in anticounterfeiting and light-emitting diode technologies. This study not only proposes a strategy to prepare photo-stimulated multicolor RTP materials, but also reveals the potential of CDs in exploiting novel optical materials with unique properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials show promising applications in optoelectronic displays, information anticounterfeiting, bioimaging, and sensing owing to their long lifetime, significant Stokes shift, abundant excited states, and high sensitivity to the environment [1–7]. Current RTP materials are usually metal-doped inorganic and organometallic compounds. However, these materials contain metals, such as Ru (II), Ir (III), Rh (III), Pt (II), etc., with high toxicity, poor stability, and high cost [8–10]. Additionally, it is challenging to obtain RTP emission for pure organic RTP materials because the spin has forbidden resistance at room temperature. This makes orbital overlap unfavorable, resulting in very low spin–orbit coupling (SOC) efficiency and a poor intersystem crossing (ISC) process from the excited singlet state (S1) to different triplet states (Tn) [11–13]. Therefore, developing a new metal-free, pure-organic RTP that is cheap, energy saving, and environmentally friendly is of great significance and application value.

To achieve pure organic RTP luminophores with a persistent phosphorescence lifetime, several factors must be fulfilled: (1) efficient spin flipping due to the ISC process, from S1 to Tn, (2) a low nonradiative relaxation rate to promote phosphorescence from T1 to S0, and (3) a slow decay of T1 to realize long-lived phosphorescence [14–18]. Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterial, have many advantages compared with traditional organic phosphorescent materials. These advantages include a simple preparation process, easy surface functionalization modification, good biocompatibility, abundant raw material sources, low cost, etc., resulting in increasing attention from researchers [19–21]. According to the literature, the aromatic carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs can effectively adjust the electronic configuration of involved single and triplet states to enhance SOC and promote the ISC process. In addition, N atoms, like O atoms, can favor n-π* leaps to increase the SOC constants by filling the triplet state excitons through ISC [22].

Researchers typically disperse CDs inside substrate-free matrices (silica gel, alumina, zeolite, layered double hydroxides, etc.) and polymers (polyvinyl alcohol, polymethyl methacrylate, and urea, etc.) to form isolated structures to obtain RTP emission by suppressing nonradiative transitions. For example, Jiang et al. successfully prepared a matrix by introducing heteroatoms to form cross-linked structures to further form matrices and successfully prepared color-tunable (500–575 nm) CDs materials [23]. Their CDs were coated with entangled carbon chains, as a substrate would inhibit the nonradiative leap process, which plays a crucial role in RTP production. Zheng et al. reported CDs that stabilize trilinear excitons by suppressing intramolecular vibrations. They constructed a rigid hydrogen bonding network framework and the longest emission wavelength was up to 618 nm with a lifetime reaching 31 ms [24]. Despite excellent achievements, the RTP of CDs is still in its infancy. Most reported CDs afterglow emission wavelengths are limited to below 600 nm and preparing long-wavelength phosphorescent emission CDs is relatively arduous. Currently, a total of sixteen reports of CDs afterglow with long emission wavelength (λem > 600 nm) exist and no more than five reports have been made where the afterglow material lifetime τp > 0.1 s. Because of the energy gap law, it is very challenging for fabricated organic afterglow materials to simultaneously achieve a long afterglow lifetime (τp > 0.1 s) and long afterglow emission wavelength (λem > 600 nm).

Herein, a facile approach is reported to synthesize CDs with urea (referred to as uCDs) that exhibit long-lived multicolor RTP under ambient conditions. RTP emission of the uCDs powder is observed to change from 570 to 610 nm, with the excitation wavelength shifting from 365 to 460 nm. Structural characterization and spectroscopic analyses demonstrate that the multicolor emission can be attributed to the existence of multiple emitting centers in the uCDs and that the hydrogen bonds formed between the uCDs play a critical role in achieving RTP. Importantly, by heat treating the uCDs at 150 °C, an increased RTP lifetime, from 91 to 281 ms, which is the longest reported lifetime for long-wavelength emission CD-based materials, is observed. Further studies demonstrate that as a rigid matrix, melted recrystallized urea protects the triplet state from a quenched long lifetime by suppressing nonradiative processes. Given their unique optical properties, a preliminary application of uCDs in advanced anticounterfeiting techniques and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) is presented.

A novel CD-based material was successfully synthesized by the hydrothermal method (details are provided in the Supporting Information). Fig. 1 provides an overview of the synthesis process. First, the luminescence properties of the CDs powder were studied by steady-state and transient spectroscopy. The luminescence spectra of the CDs were excited with different wavelengths and the results show that the change of luminescence spectrum is almost constant, which indicates that the CDs do not have excitation dependence. Through characterization of the CDs, it was found that their surface is rich in –CNH, CO, and –OH groups. Notably, the CDs have no RTP emission in both liquid and solid state after cessation of ultraviolet (UV) lamp irradiation. We propose the following possible mechanism for CDs that do not exhibit RTP emission (Fig. S4).

The CDs show an aggregated state in molecules and electrons populate the S0 state to the S1 state under 365 nm excitation. Triplet excitons generated by excitation of the CDs mainly lose energy through nonradiative relaxation processes, which are not sufficient to stabilize the triplet state and result in CDs unable to achieve RTP.

To make afterglow occur, uCDs powder was obtained by treating the CDs with urea. To determine uCDs properties, a series of characterization measurements were carried out on powder samples. First, the morphology of the prepared uCDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs material showed a well dispersed quasi-spherical shape and were successfully encapsulated in a urea matrix, with an average uCDs size of 3.25 nm, after modification with urea (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 2b) show that the uCDs have excellent lattice stripes with lattice spacings of 0.19 and 0.21 nm, which are attributed to the (100) plane of graphite [25]. Therefore, we conclude that the core of the uCDs is a graphite structure.

As can be observed from the X-ray diffraction spectrum in Fig. 2d, the peak positions of the uCDs almost overlap those of molten urea, further confirming that the CDs are encapsulated and homogeneously embedded in the urea matrix after urea modification. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows 1464, 1564, 1681, 3347, and 3443 cm−1 absorption peaks belonging to C–N, CO, CN, O–H, and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively [26]. This indicates that the surface of the uCDs contain abundant groups, such as –CNH, CO, and –OH. CDs are embedded in the matrix by binding to urea and may be held together through hydrogen bonding. To further confirm the FT-IR analyses, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted for the uCDs. XPS (Fig. 2f) experiments indicate that uCDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O, with respective elemental contents of C: 43%, N: 35%, and O: 22%. The increased essential content of N in uCDs compared to CDs is consistent with their encapsulation in urea, as shown in the uCDs TEM images. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2g) indicates the presence of C–C/CC (284.4 eV), C–N (286.1 eV), and OC–O (288.7 eV). The XPS N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2h) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.2 and 399.7 eV, which are attributed to C– NC and N–H, respectively. These results confirm FT-IR results that the CDs are embedded in the urea matrix through hydrogen bonding. Combining the above discussion, we speculate that there may be a large number of C- and heteroatom-containing functional groups on the surface of the uCDs that inhibit nonradiative transitions.

Subsequently, we systematically investigated the photophysical properties of solid-state uCDs. As shown in Fig. S5a, the uCDs photoluminescence spectra show a shift slight of the emission peak with change of excitation wavelength. The emission maxima shift from about 546 to 570 nm when the excitation wavelength is changed from 365 to 460 nm. This indicates that uCDs might have excitation dependence. Notably, when the excitation source (365 and 460 nm) is switched off, the uCDs show an afterglow phenomenon under ambient conditions (Fig. 3 a). To explore this emission behavior, we measured the corresponding spectra of uCDs at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). As the excitation wavelength changes from 365 nm to 460 nm, the RTP color of the uCDs powder also shows a red-shift phenomenon, with the RTP emission position shifting from around 570 nm to approximately 610 nm. These wavelengths are consistence with the RTP color and indicate that the RTP color of the uCDs is tunable. As expected, after urea modification, uCDs exhibited luminescence emission under UV-lamp excitation. The uCDs produce phosphorescence emission due to the possible formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and urea, which inhibits the nonradiative transition.

The photoluminescence spectrum is not consistent with the phosphorescence spectrum emission wavelength, which can be proven to be RTP emission. Based on equation (1) [27,28], we calculated the energy gap (ΔEST) between the triplet (T1) and single (S1) states at 365 and 460 nm excitation wavelengths:
(1)
Δ E
S T
=
1024
λ
P L
−
1024
λ
p h o s
resulting in values of 0.05 and 0.16 eV, respectively. These narrow values indicate a practical ISC process for three-state excitation from the S1 state to the T1 state. To determine the RTP lifetimes of the uCDs, we measured the time-resolved spectrum of the uCDs powders at 365 and 460 nm excitation wavelengths. These decay spectra were then fitted with a triple exponential function. Based on the following equation [29,30]:
(2)
τ
a v g
= Σ
α i
τ i 2
/ Σ
α i
τ i
the average RTP lifetimes were calculated to be approximately 381 and 91 ms for excitation wavelengths of 365 and 460 nm, respectively (Fig. 3d). The color of the RTP emission can be quantitatively described by the coherent infrared energy (CIE) coordinates (Fig. 3e), which vary from (0.42, 0.48) to (0.45, 0.43) with CCTs of 3921 and 3035 K, respectively, and correspond well with the photo in Fig. 1.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the UV–vis spectrum of the uCDs has an absorption peak at 330 nm and a broad absorption band spanning from 300 to 400 nm, which is general assigned to the n-π* leap of CO/CN [31–33]. The change of absorption spectrum is due to the enrichment of CDs surface functional groups by the introduction of urea, which then affects the photoluminescence emission of the CDs. The absorption spectrum confirms that the surface of the uCDs has complex functional groups and many emission centers, so there is a possibility of afterglow emission with variable wavelength. According to previous reports [34], the excitation-wavelength-dependent RTP emission and the tri-exponential decay feature imply that the uCDs have multiple emission centers.

Although RTP emission occurs after 600 nm, it has a short lifetime. The uCDs were treated with water by heating to obtain uCDs@150 samples and their solid-state photophysical properties were investigated. After the reheating treatment, a longer afterglow time can be observed by the unaided eye. The uCDs@150 powder exhibits similar emission under 365–460 nm UV-light excitation. The emission peaks shift slightly with changing excitation wavelength (Fig. S5b). Surprisingly, the emission position of the photoluminescence spectrum was almost unchanged after the reheating treatment. When the excitation source was switched off (Fig. 4 a), the uCDs@150 sample RTP color changed from yellow-green to orange. The phosphorescent spectrum of uCDs@150 (Fig. 4b) shows a red-shifted emission position, from 565 nm to 605 nm, which is in excellent agreement with the RTP colors.

The TEM images of uCDs@150 in Fig. S6a show that the CDs are successfully encapsulated in the composite matrix (the melting recrystallization urea and biuret from the heating urea) and crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 2.35 nm is observed (Fig. S6b) [35,36]. The corresponding XRD spectrum (Fig. S7a) of uCDs@150 shows that the uCDs undergo melt recrystallization upon heating and the fused urea can adhere firmly to the CDs upon evaporation. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S7b), the characteristic peaks of the uCDs@150 at 3414 cm−1 and 1690 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and C–N, respectively, and are significantly weaker at 1564 cm−1 compared to unheated uCDs. The apparent weakening of the characteristic peak at 1564 cm−1 is probably due to recrystallization phenomenon at high temperatures, which distributes the CDs in a rigid environment with reduced C–O content. As shown in Fig. S7c, the UV–vis spectrum of the uCDs@150 has an absorption peak at 330 nm and a broad absorption band spanning from 300 to 400 nm, which is general assigned to the n-π* leap of CO/CN. As shown in Fig. S8, the XPS spectra show that uCDs@150 is mainly composed of C, N, and O with 40%, 28%, and 32% content of each element, respectively. Compared to unheated uCDs, the C and N element content increases and the O 1s spectrum shows a decrease in C–O content, which is consistent with the FT-IR results. The smaller CDs size after heating, the reduced C–O bond content, and a more ordered graphite structure alter the CDs band gap, resulting in a significant blue shift in the emission peak of the uCDs@150 sample. In a further set of experiments, we investigated the eﬀects of temperature on the photoluminescence of uCDs and uCDs@150 powders in the range of 77 and 300 K (Figure S9). There is an initial rapid decay of phosphorescence is almost identical in the range of 77 and 300 K, which indicates the very eﬀective nature of the hydrogen bonds in suppressing vibrational dissipation at all temperatures [36,37]. The biuret comes from urea during the heating process, as confirmed by the FTIR spectrum (Figure S10). There is a large number of amino groups on biuret molecules, so we suggest that these groups could eﬀectively form hydrogen bonds to rigidify CN/CO bonds on uCDs, leading to eﬀective phosphorescence emission. This result is consistent with previous results from phosphorescence spectroscopy. The results of the time-resolved spectra (Fig. 4c) were calculated from Equation (2). The luminescence lifetime of the uCDs@150 were 445 and 281 ms at excitation wavelengths of 365 and 460 nm, respectively. The luminescence intensity at 365 nm and 460 nm excitation increased by nearly 4 and 2 times, respectively. Long afterglow lifetime and high luminescence intensity are achieved via heat treatment of the uCDs at 150 °C.

Based on the above results, we propose a mechanism by which afterglow intensity is enhanced after reheating of uCDs. First, after urea modification of CDs (Fig. 5 a), hydrogen bond interactions form between CDs that suppress the intermolecular vibration and fix the triplet exciton so that uCDs show weak RTP phenomenon. When the uCDs are heat treated (Fig. 5b), the urea further crystallizes to form a rigid matrix. Composite matrices play a dual role to suppress the vibrational dissipation of long-lived triplets by combining the rigidity of the melting recrystallization urea and hydrogen bonding between biuret and uCDs, to enhance uCDs phosphorescence. Therefore, with changing excitation wavelength, some emission centers will dominate the RTP process and endow solid-state uCDs@150 with color-tunable phosphorescence [24,25].

Given the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of uCDs@150, we further demonstrate its potential application in information security and LEDs. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the pattern “NYNU” drawn with uCDs@150 markers can be easily observed with the naked eye as yellow–green and orange phosphorescence after excitation with UV light at 365 and 460 nm. This effect can be implemented as an anti-counterfeit encryption material with practical applications. Fig. 6b shows LEDs prepared from uCDs@150-composite coated LEDs and bright phosphorescent emission is still observably by the naked eye when the applied voltage is switched off. As the LED excitation wavelength changes, the light emission color also changes. This shows that the prepared RTP materials have potential for lighting applications. These advantages offer the potential application of uCDs@150 composites for future optoelectronic and cryogenic electronics.

In summary, a facile approach for the preparation of long-life multicolor RTP CDs is proposed. CDs repeat heat-treated in urea (uCDs) change RTP emission wavelength (570 nm–610 nm) under different irradiation wavelengths. This phenomenon can be attributed to the existence of multiple emission centers in uCDs and the stabilization of triplet excitons by hydrogen bonds. The RTP lifetime of the prepared uCDs@150 was increased from 91 ms to 281 ms by an additional heat treatment at 150 °C. This increase in lifetime was attributed to the formation of a rigid matrix by melting recrystallized urea, which inhibits the nonradiative process and avoids triplet-state quenching. Based on its unique optical properties, uCDs@150 can be applied to advanced anticounterfeiting and LED technologies. Our findings not only provide design ideas for multicolor RTP materials but also have potential for the development of optical materials with unique properties.

C.Li:Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. H.Liang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. S.C.Bai: Visualization, Investigation. J.Y.Zhu:Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Z.J.Chen:Software, Supervision, Validation. G.Yang: Resources, Visualization. Y.S.Zhu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U1904178, 62104119, U2004145), Natural Science Foundation of Henan (No. 202300410295), Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics (No. IOSKL2020KF08).
